body:
Nervous System Regulation After Solar Plexus Emotions

You're probably reading this because you're in a dark place, aren't you? Maybe it's 3 AM, and you're staring at the ceiling, heart pounding, stomach churning, wondering if you're losing your mind. That deep, gnawing anxiety in your solar plexus - it's not just a feeling, it's a physical sensation that's taken over your whole being. You're not alone. I've been there. I know that terror, that feeling of being completely unmoored, like your insides are being ripped out and rearranged, and you have no say in it. It's rough. Truly rough.

Here's the thing: what you're experiencing, especially if you've been dabbling in spiritual practices, meditation, or even just going through a really intense period of growth, could be something called spiritual emergency. And yeah, it's a hell of an emergency. It feels like your nervous system has gone rogue, right? Like it's screaming at you, constantly. That burning, twisting sensation in your gut - that's your solar plexus chakra, your power center, your emotional core, screaming for attention, or maybe just screaming in pain. It's a lot. I get it.

Look, I'm not here to tell you it's all sunshine and rainbows. It's not. This kind of experience can feel like a personal hell. But I'm also here to tell you that you're not broken, and you're not going crazy. What's happening is a deep reorganization, and your nervous system is just trying to keep up. It's like your internal wiring is being upgraded, but the electrician forgot to tell you about the temporary power outages and sparks. You know? So yeah, let's talk about how to get through this, how to bring some semblance of peace back to that churning gut of yours.

Understanding What's Happening in Your Gut

That feeling in your solar plexus - it's not just "stress." It's deeper. It's often where we store unprocessed emotions, old traumas, fears, and anxieties. When you start to awaken spiritually, or when kundalini energy begins to move, it acts like a powerful solvent. It stirs up all that old gunk. Imagine a muddy pond. When you stir it up, the water gets even murkier before it settles and clears. That's what's happening inside you. Your system is trying to process and release what's been stuck there, sometimes for years, sometimes for lifetimes.

For me, it felt like a constant knot, a burning ball of dread that never left. I couldn't eat, I couldn't sleep, I couldn't focus. Every little thing felt like a threat. My body was in a constant state of fight-or-flight. Does that sound familiar? It's your nervous system in overdrive. It's interpreting all this internal upheaval as a mortal danger, and it's reacting accordingly. It's not a conscious choice; it's a primal survival mechanism. And it's exhausting. Absolutely draining.

The Kundalini Connection

If you've been exploring spiritual paths, you might have heard of kundalini. It's often described as a dormant energy at the base of the spine that, when awakened, rises through the chakras, leading to deep spiritual experiences. Sounds great, right? Well, it can be. But for many - and I'm talking about a lot of us - it can also be incredibly destabilizing. That's where books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) become invaluable. They offer a glimpse into the raw, often terrifying, reality of this process. Sannella documented cases that sound exactly like what you might be going through - intense heat, involuntary movements, extreme anxiety, and yes, that stomach-churning fear.

When kundalini energy hits the manipura chakra (your solar plexus), it can feel like an explosion. All the suppressed anger, fear, shame, and powerlessness stored there can come flooding to the surface. It's not a gentle trickle; it's often a torrent. Your nervous system isn't prepared for that kind of energetic discharge. It's like trying to run too much electricity through old wiring - things short circuit. And that's what those intense physical and emotional sensations are: your system trying to cope with an overload.

Spiritual Emergency vs. Mental Illness

This is a big one, and it's where a lot of us get lost. You might be convinced you're having a mental breakdown. And honestly, some of the symptoms can look similar to anxiety disorders, depression, or even psychosis. That's why it's so important to find someone who understands spiritual emergency. Stanislav and Christina Grof, in their book The Stormy Search for the Self (paid link), were pioneers in recognizing these states as distinct from mental illness. They argued that while the symptoms can be distressing, they're part of a natural, albeit intense, process of psychological and spiritual transformation.

The difference is often in the underlying context and the direction of the experience. Are you feeling completely disconnected, with no sense of meaning or purpose, and your reality is crumbling without any new insights emerging? Or, despite the terror, is there also a sense of something deep happening, a feeling that you're shedding old layers, even if it feels like you're dying in the process? It's a fine line, I know. And sometimes, you need professional help to figure it out. Don't be afraid to seek that out. Seriously. There's no shame in it. Sometimes you need a guide who's seen this before, who can help you distinguish between a genuine spiritual unfolding and something else that needs a different kind of care.

Practical Steps to Soothe Your System

Okay, so you know what's happening. Now what? How do you calm that churning storm in your gut? It's not about stopping the process - you can't, not really - but it's about learning to surf the waves instead of getting dragged under. It's about bringing regulation back to your nervous system, even when it feels impossible. These aren't quick fixes, but they're practices that, over time, can make a huge difference. I've used them, and I've seen them work for others.

  • Grounding, Grounding, Grounding: When you're overwhelmed, you're probably dissociated, floating somewhere outside your body. You need to get back in. What does that mean?
    • Physical contact: Stand barefoot on the earth. Seriously. Grass, dirt, sand - whatever you can find. Feel the texture, the temperature. It sounds simple, but it's incredibly powerful.
    • Sensory input: Hold an ice cube. Splash cold water on your face. Listen intently to the sounds around you - not just generally, but pick out individual sounds. Look at specific objects and name their colors, shapes, and textures. Engage your five senses to pull you back into the present moment.
    • Movement: Gentle movement. Not intense exercise if you're already agitated. Think walking, slow stretching, tai chi, or even just rocking gently. Movement helps discharge excess energy and brings you back into your body.
  • Breathwork (Gentle, Slow, and Controlled): When you're in panic mode, your breath is probably shallow and fast. You need to slow it down. But don't try anything too intense right now. Forget those fancy pranayama techniques for a bit.
    • Diaphragmatic breathing: Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. As you inhale, let your belly rise. As you exhale, let it fall. Your chest should move minimally. This signals to your vagus nerve that you're safe.
    • Exhale longer than inhale: Try inhaling for a count of 4, holding for 1, and exhaling for a count of 6 or 8. The longer exhale activates your parasympathetic nervous system - your "rest and digest" mode. Do this for just a few minutes, several times a day. It can feel impossible at first, but stick with it.
  • Safe Space Creation: Your environment matters. A lot. Create a space where you feel as safe and calm as possible.
    • Declutter: A cluttered physical space often mirrors a cluttered mental space. Clear it out.
    • Comfort: Soft blankets, calming scents (lavender, chamomile), dim lighting. Whatever makes you feel cozy and protected.
    • Sound: Gentle, calming music, nature sounds, or even just silence. Avoid anything jarring or overstimulating.

Befriending Your Inner Experience

This is probably the hardest part, because everything in you wants to run from these sensations. You want them to stop. You want them to go away. But here's the truth: fighting them often makes them stronger. It's like trying to push a beach ball underwater - the harder you push, the harder it pops back up. The way out is often through, but not through brute force, through gentle, compassionate witnessing.

The Practice of Allowing

When that familiar knot of anxiety hits your solar plexus, your first instinct is to distract yourself, to numb out, to escape. Don't. Not entirely. Instead, try to turn towards it, just for a moment. This isn't about wallowing in it; it's about acknowledging it. Say to yourself, "Okay, here it is again. This feeling of dread. This tightness." Don't judge it. Don't label it as "bad." Just observe it. Where exactly is it in your body? What's its texture? Its temperature? Does it move? Does it have a color?

This isn't easy. It takes courage. But what you're doing is creating a tiny bit of space between "you" and "the feeling." You're realizing you're not the feeling; you're the one experiencing the feeling. This is a subtle but incredibly powerful shift. It starts to disarm the fear response, because you're no longer completely identified with the terror. You're observing it, which implies you're separate from it. Know what I mean? It's like watching a scary movie versus being in the scary movie.

Compassionate Inquiry

As you allow the feeling to be there, you can gently ask it questions. Not with your logical mind, but with an open heart. "What do you need?" "What are you trying to tell me?" "Where did you come from?" Don't expect immediate answers. Sometimes, images or memories will surface. Sometimes, a deep sadness will emerge. Just let it be there. Hold it gently, as you would a scared child. This is where you start to integrate. You're not pushing it away; you're bringing it into the light of your awareness. Bonnie Greenwell's books, like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide (paid link), are fantastic resources for this kind of inner work. She talks extensively about the need for compassionate self-witnessing and integration during these intense periods.

Support Systems and Professional Help

You can't do this alone. And honestly, you shouldn't have to. This isn't a solo journey, even though it often feels incredibly isolating. Finding the right support is absolutely vital for your sanity and your recovery.

  • Therapists and Coaches Who "Get It": This is probably the most challenging part. Not every therapist understands spiritual emergency or kundalini. Many will pathologize it. You need someone who has experience with these phenomena, or at least an open mind and a willingness to learn. Look for therapists specializing in "spiritual emergence," "transpersonal psychology," or "somatic experiencing." Ask direct questions: "Do you have experience with spiritual crises or kundalini awakenings?" If they look at you blankly, move on. It's okay. Keep looking.
  • Support Groups: Online forums, local groups (if you can find them) - connecting with others who are going through similar experiences can be a lifeline. Just knowing you're not the only one can reduce the isolation and shame significantly. Be discerning, though. Some groups can be unhelpful or even reinforce fear. Find a community that feels genuinely supportive and grounded.
  • Trusted Friends and Family: Choose wisely. Not everyone will understand. Some will tell you to "just snap out of it" or suggest you're crazy. Protect your energy. Share with those who can listen without judgment, who can offer practical support (like making you a meal or sitting with you), and who genuinely care about your well-being, even if they don't fully grasp what you're experiencing.
  • Bodyworkers and Energy Healers: Sometimes, conventional talk therapy isn't enough. Bodywork like massage, acupuncture, craniosacral therapy, or reiki can help release trapped energy and calm the nervous system directly. Just make sure you find reputable practitioners.

Patience and Self-Compassion

This isn't a race. There's no finish line you cross and suddenly everything is perfect. This is a process of integration, and it takes time. Sometimes a lot of time. You'll have good days and bad days. You'll feel like you're making progress, and then you'll hit a wall and feel like you're back at square one. That's normal. That's part of it. Don't beat yourself up for it.

Be kind to yourself. You're going through something incredibly intense. Give yourself permission to rest, to cry, to not be productive, to just be. Treat yourself with the same tenderness you would a very sick or frightened child. Because in a way, that's what you are right now - a part of you is very scared and needs a lot of love and reassurance. Celebrate the small victories - a full night's sleep, a moment of peace, a meal you actually enjoyed. They add up. They really do.

You're strong. Stronger than you think. You're still here, still fighting, still seeking answers in the middle of the night. That's resilience. Keep going. You'll find your way through this. I promise you, it does get better. It truly does. It might not look like you expect, but you will find peace again. You'll find a new kind of strength. Just keep breathing, keep allowing, and keep seeking that gentle regulation. You've got this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
